original_abstract: Optical neural networks (ONNs) offer a promising pathway toward high-speed, energy-efficient artificial intelligence hardware by leveraging the massive parallelism of light. However, scaling these architectures while achieving high-level integration and operational precision remains a significant challenge. In this work, we demonstrate a fully integrated photonic processing unit (PPU) driven by an on-chip AlGaAs-on-insulator dark-pulse microcomb. By implementing a convolution scheme based on time-wavelength plane stretching, we enable high-speed parallel data processing within a compact footprint. To overcome the sensitivities inherent in integrated analog photonics, we developed a specialized control and calibration protocol, achieving a record weight precision of 9 bits. The resulting system exhibits a preeminent photonic-core compute density of 1.04 trillion operations per second per square millimeter (TOPS mm⁻²), outperforming previous wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) ONN architectures by a factor of five. We validate the PPU through proof-of-concept demonstrations of image edge detection and handwritten digit recognition, achieving classification accuracies comparable to digital computers. This highly scalable approach marks a transition for parallel photonic accelerators from laboratory prototypes toward practical, high-performance deployment in fields such as autonomous driving and real-time video analysis.
humanized_abstract: Optical neural networks (ONNs) are considered as one of the feasible solutions for the implementation of fast and low power consumption artificial intelligence systems using the high parallel processing characteristics of light. However, the large-scale integration and high precision operation is still a major challenge for the realization of ONNs. In this paper, a fully integrated photonic processing unit (PPU) has been developed using an on-chip driven AlGaAs-on-insulator dark-pulse microcomb, and high speed parallel data processing is realized by utilizing a convolution operation based on time-wavelength plane stretching. The integrated analog photonics is highly sensitive, so a special control and calibration protocol was established, and a record weight precision of 9 bits was obtained. Thus, the system exhibits the maximum photonic-core compute density of 1.04 TOPS mm⁻², which is 5 times more than that of the wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) ONNs, and the corresponding PPU is used for image edge detection and handwritten digit recognition, and the average classification accuracies of the corresponding digital computers are maintained. The highly scalable system promotes the parallel photonic accelerators from the laboratory development to the practical high performance applications, such as autonomous driving and real-time video recognition.

The highly scalable system promotes the parallel photonic accelerators from the laboratory development to the practical high performance applications, such as autonomous driving and real-time video recognition.
